sentences.text: My name is Alicia Appleman.
And my maiden name is Jurman.
Appleman, A-P-P-L-E-M-A-N.
And my maiden name, J-U-R-M-A-N.
Alicia.
In polish, it this Alicia.
Yes.
From Alicia, you can call me Ala, A-L-A, or Alusia, A-L-U-S-I-A.
I will be 66 in May.
I was born in a little village called Rosulna R-O-S-L-N-A.
And it's in the Carpathian mountains in Poland.
And from the age of five, I lived in the city of Buczacz, B-U-C-Z-A-C-Z.
And it's also in Poland.
My father's name was Sigmund Jurman.
And he was a former officer in World War I.
And he was born in Vienna in Austria.
My mother's name was Frieda.
And she was born in Sweden.
They spoke three languages at home, Hebrew, German, and Polish.
I had four brothers, my oldest brother Zachary.
He was born in 1922.
My second brother Moshe was born in 1924.
And then there was Bunio.
And he was born in 1928.
I was born in 1930.
And I had a little brother Hertzl, who was born in 1932.
Well, I tell the students when I speak in schools that we were a very active family.
We lived in the mountains.
My father built us a big house, put the pool around it, put a couple of drawbridges.
And I felt like a princess.
I had three older brothers who bossed me around, as older brothers do.
I also had a younger brother, whom I bossed around, as older sisters do.
I remember my father telling me that he would have had as many boys as need be until he had his darling daughter Alicia.
Well, I was a very active little girl.
We lived already in the vacationing home.
So we actually received a lot of people.
But in the summer we used to go and berry picking, as far as I remember when we lived in the mountains.
But then when be moved over to Buczacz, I spent most of my vacations in my uncle's home, Dr.
Kurtz, Stanislalow.
Because from to age of five, I wanted to be a physician like my uncle.
So I spent my summers mostly there.
Because we went to the river to swim, most of my years up till the war, I spent my summers there and visiting my grandparents who lived in the mountains.
My grandfather, Kurtz, Opa, was born in Sweden.
And he owns forests and lumber yards in Solotvyne, in [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], all in that area.
He came before World War I.
They had 16 children, 11 daughters and 5 sons.
And they came to what was then the Austrian Hungarian empire because Grandpa needed 11 sons-in-law for his 11 daughters.
He was a very wealthy man.
But he was also a very kind man.
I remember my grandfather used to, in the spring, when he used to get the lumber on the river.
And he used to wave at us when we were children standing on the bank of the river.
And what I remember, when I was about five years old, in the wintertime they mark the trees, the lumber that's going to be cut down.
They had a big room.
And then they had a fire in the center.
My grandfather threw me over the fire for somebody to catch me.
And I was so happy.
I wasn't afraid at all.
And then I remember one day he came to our house.
And he says, Alicia, you see, take this little basket.
Take this little pen.
And I want you to show me how you wash your underwear?
And I said, grandpa, how can you say something.
I say, I want you to do that.
Because one day you're going to be in charge of a big estate.
And I want you to know how to do everything as well as the people who will been working for you.
Now, he was a very lovely man.
And of course, Grandma was a wonderfully lady.
I loved Grandma too.
My mother's My mother is from the house of Kurtz.
There were two things I remember very well about my mother.
When I was about seven years old-- actually, from the age of five, in our tradition for Shabbat you bring food to the needy people.
We didn't use the expression poor people, needy people.
We shared.
We didn't give.
And since I was the daughter of the house, it was my honor to bring the food to the needy people.
And I started on Wednesday after Hebrew school.
And one day my friend Rachel came to me.
She was all excited.
She says, Alicia, I have a secret to tell you.
Could your maid bring the food in for this one Friday, this one-- it was on Thursday-- this one afternoon?
Please, could you ask your mother?
So I came to my mother all excited.
Who doesn't want to hear a secret?
And my mother sat me down and says, Alicia, darling, I would like to share something with you.
She says, you know, God gave you the greatest of gifts.
And that's the ability to make a decision.
Now, you're going to make a decision today.
You can go and bring the food to the people or you can go and have your secret with Rachel.
Whichever way you will decide, I will honor it.
The decision is yours.
And  of course, when I looked at my mother's beautiful eyes, I was going to bring the food and postpone Rachel.

But this was implanted in my heart so deeply, because later on during the war when I saw people making decisions of killing us, or any other decisions, or decisions I had to make, I was always sure that the decision is mine.
I can make a decision.
Whichever way I go, the decision is mine.
Well, in Europe you are committed, if you have the means, to be your brother's keeper-- so you belong to an organization.
You take care of orphans.
You take care of old people.
You are doing a lot of good deeds.
And of course, we went to the temple.
Our holidays were very beautiful.
And we children went to Hebrew school.
We spoke Hebrew.
We learned Hebrew from the very beginning.
I think I spoke Hebrew from the cradle.
It was just something wonderful to be, to be a Jewish girl, and to go to Professor Farenhoff's Academy, Hebrew Academy, and to celebrate the holidays, and to do good deeds.
That was one of the important things.
Our home burned down.
And in Europe, if you have an orchard, you lay your fruit in long cases, wooden cases in the straw to ripen during the wintertime.
And the girl-- there was a mother and girl who lived in our house.
We didn't have electricity there.
So she went to get some fruit.
And she got it from the attic.
She left the candle burning there.
And the candle tipped over into the straw, ignited, started a fire.
But what really blew the house up is-- my father had the car which was parked on the foot of the hill.
But the gasoline was on the top of the hill.
And the gasoline blew up.
And that was Friday night when my parents were in synagogue.
So the girl saved the children, saved us.
But the house, and my father's business, everything was blown up, just went very quickly.
And I remember watching it.
We moved to Buczacz in 1935.
At the beginning, I was very lonely for the mountains.
And at the beginning, we lived in a rented home.
And then father, he built us a home.
And I made friends.
I went to-- in Poland, should go to a Catholic school because Poland is a Catholic country.
But in the afternoon, I went to Hebrew school every single day for three hours.
And I loved every moment of it.
I learned how to read and write Hebrew.
Of course, I learned all about the traditions.
I was very proud of my heritage.
And I made friends.
Buczacz is a very lovely town.
It has the river Strypa.
And of course, my brothers played soccer.
And I was very deeply involved with them because I followed my brother wherever he went to the games.
And we had friends.
Life was very sweet for us.
I was a very happy girl.
I was a very good student in both schools, in the Polish school and in the Hebrew school.
Of course, I could have-- I would have liked to see my father a little bit more because he traveled in order to make a living.
But otherwise, I was very happy with my brothers, and my mother, and my father.
Zachary was a musician.
He was a violinist.
He was a tall boy.
He was blonde, blue-eyed.
He took after my father.
He was very kind.
He loved children.
And I adored my brother.
I simply adored him.
Then later when he was in school, he wrote to me.
And then he came for the summer vacation.
I was following him around.
He was like a father for me because my father wasn't there.
Moshe had brown hair and brown eyes, like myself.
He wanted to be a forest engineer like grandfather.
He was very good in chess.
He was a scientist.
He wanted to be a scientist.
He was reserved.
And he was very lovely.
Now, Bunio was like Zachary.
But he was younger.
And I loved him very much.
And he was also a very good student.
And Hertzl, of course, was the one I bossed around.
He looked like my brother, Zachary.
He was blonde, blue-eyed, and a very good little boy.
When my mother gave us chocolate, for example-- and she divided it into two halves.
I ate my chocolate quickly.
And then I stood in front of my brother with a very unhappy face.
And then he says, Alicia, you want a little piece of mine?
And so he gave.
I was a meanie.
And he gave me half of his chocolate.
It wasn't very nice to do it.
But he was a very kind little boy, very kind.
Oh, this is very interesting.
I didn't really have problems because I didn't really know much about the anti-Semitism until I was eight years old.
And my brother came back from the Conservatory of Music very badly beaten.
They beat him up, destroyed his violin.
He went to the Conservatory of Music in Lvov, which is now Russia.
And you spell it L-V-O-V.
And that's when I found the first time about the anti-Semitism.
But I had a very good friend.
And her name was Slavka.
She was a Ukrainian girl.
And we became very good friends.
When I first came to Buczacz, I was looking for skiing and for a little hill.
In the city, there weren't many hills.
And I found this wonderful little hill.
And I was skiing down.
Except this hill had one disadvantage.
It went straight into the main street.
So if you skied, you really had to brake, not to get into the farmers' sleighs.
You could get yourself killed.
And as I was turning back to go up the hill, I saw a skier without ski polls just going down like that.
And I knew she was into trouble because she didn't sound like an experienced skier.
But she went down that hill.
And I made myself a shield.
And I caught her.
So we fell over.
And then a sleigh passed us at that time.
And so the farmer was very angry.
And he gave us a couple of switches.
And we became-- that was Slavka.
The reason she went down the hill, her cousin challenged her.
And she was about six years old.
And we became very good friends.
It affected me very deeply.
I was very shattered because I could not understand anybody hurting my brother because he was such a wonderful and kind boy.
But what happened is when he came home and he was healed, my father took him into the meadow and trained him how to fight, really trained him.
And I followed for a whole week.
I was not in school.
I hid in the bushes.
And I watched my father train my brother.
And I picked up a few good points.
And so then he went back for the school year of 1938-- that happened in 1938, he wrote a letter to my parents telling them that he was fine.
They fell upon him again.
But this time he knew how to defend himself.
And he left them a couple of good punches just to remember him by.
And he wrote a letter to me.
Dear, Alicia, I hope you didn't get into too much troubles with your teacher when our father trained us.
So he knew I was there.
It didn't make any difference between me and my teachers, or between me and my friend Slavka, or my other friends.
But I did have one problem when I was in the fourth grade.
We had a math teacher.
And she was quite anti-Semitic.
Whenever she called the Jewish students to the blackboard, she intimidated them.
They knew the answers.
They knew their math.
But they got so frightened.
Because in my time, the teacher used the ruler to hit the children on their hands.
So I used to whisper the answers to them in Hebrew.
And the teacher finally found out that she expelled me from my class.
So my friend Slavka said to me, Alicia, why don't you teach me the numbers in Hebrew and I will whisper in your place?
But later on, she was committing.
She was committing a crime-- not the crime, but she was doing something wrong.
She had to go to her priest and tell him about it.
So I went with her.
And I waited outside the church until she gave her report to the priest and he forgave her.
She was so wonderful.
And one day we had the holiday of the trees, like [NON-ENGLISH].
And she came carrying her little tree.
She also wanted to plant a tree.
So we took her along.
Children don't know hate.
Your children just know love.
The last Jewish holiday, it was 1941.
And that was-- I think my father wasn't alive.
It was the high holidays, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
But I don't think my father was alive at the time.
We didn't have the synagogue open anymore.
The Germans were there already.
They came September 1, 1941.
No.
They came June 6.
The German came in the summer, I think June 6, the summer of 1941.
About the Russians when the war started?
The Russians came to us in 1939.
The Second World War started in 1931, '39 when the Germans invaded Poland.
But the Russians also invaded Poland.
So they divided Poland into two halves.
Western Poland fell under the German occupation.
Eastern Poland fell under the Russian occupation.
So when I was nine years old, the Russians invaded my hometown.
I remember it very distinctly.
I went to the city, the center, to see what was happening.
And I saw very happy soldiers.
The Russian soldiers were very happy, very friendly to the children.
They gave us little enamel stars, red enamel stars.
And I collected some of them and brought them home.
But what they did, however, they closed our Hebrew Academy.
So we had to go underground.
Then we moved our Hebrew studies from home to home.
Our parents has to stand watch so that we shouldn't be found out by the Russian police.
They would have taken the parents, probably everybody to Siberia.
I went to the Russian school for two years.
What happened to me there, however, was very painful at one time.
I was a very good student.
I graduated, finished the school year with excellence-- they got their diploma, which was in Russian is said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] with this particular diploma which had Stalin's and Lenin's picture.
And when we had assembly at the end of the school year, the teacher mentioned my name.
Yes, Alicia Jurman is entitled to the diploma.
But because her father is an enemy of the Soviet Union, she cannot get this diploma.
And I was shamed in front of the whole school body.
I couldn't get this diploma because my father was an artist.
But he was also a businessman.
So I wasn't given this diploma.
I tried to study and tried to do my very best.
But I was considered through my family that we weren't quite what you would call kosher.
We had passport for three months, renewable each three months.
And I believe the Russians would have sent us to Siberia if it were not for my uncle.
They needed him at the hospital as a physician.
And he claimed us as his family.
So they let us stay in our home.
I think a lot of people, I remember we stayed home.
But there were some people who went to welcome them.
They were people who belong to the communist party in our hometown.
And they came to welcome the Russians.
And I didn't consider them-- my parents didn't consider them dangerous as somebody who's going to do any harm to the Jewish people in particular.
But of course, my father was aware of what communists are like.
We children, I didn't know much about it.
I only knew that I will have to learn a new language.
And we learned Russian.
And we didn't have books at the beginning.
The teacher gave us written pages.
And we had to understand what she was writing.
It was not a very happy time because unfortunately, our tragedy started already under the Russian occupation.
You see, the Russians encouraged students to study.
In Leningrad they opened a special institute there.
And my brother Moshe reasoned if he were to study in Russia, maybe it will protect us from being exiled to Siberia.
And of course, there were arguments at home because my parents didn't want him to go.
But eventually, he won them over.
He went to study to Russia.
But the conditions were so bad there that he escaped.

And when he came home about three weeks later, the Russian NKVD they came to our home, arrested my brother, and took him to prison, not in my hometown of Buczacz, but about 30 kilometers to the city of Chortkiv, which is spelled C-H-I-R-T-K-O-V.
And he died in prison there very soon.
So the tragedy started already.
And he was only 15 years old.
They kept him in prison so that he couldn't tell other boys what that school was like in Russia.
But he didn't commit a crime.
He was just homesick.
Yes.
They came to get him.
It was 1941, three weeks after he came home.
Oh, he looked very bad.
He was starving.
We sent him packages.
But they didn't give it to them.
And then besides being in school, they were also working on farms.
So he was very desperate to get home.
And he wasn't feeling well.
He was quite ill.
But he didn't have any special disease, like tuberculosis, which usually a youngster like that would get.
He was just exhausted.
I don't know how he got home.
I never heard the whole story.
But the time when they came for him, it was a terrible night, of course.
The NKVD, as we know them now as the KGB, any criminal acts they committed, or arrested was always the middle of the night to frighten people and to bring them into shock.
But my father followed my brother and went with him to Chortkiv, and pleaded with them to let them go.
He didn't say anything, not a word.
But they didn't trust him.
So he had to pay with his life.
As I mentioned before, he was a very good chess player.
In prison, he made chess figurines out of bread.
And so the commander of the prison send us a letter.
In these figurines, he said my brother lived, he would have been a chess champion.
They let us know.
The prison commander wrote to us.
But they wouldn't let us bring him home for burial.
He had to be buried there.
It's ironic.
Because I was later under the German occupation imprisoned in the same prison and thrown to be buried at the same cemetery when they beat me to death.
And I was thinking at the time that my brother Moshe is buried here someplace.
It's interesting.
Because it seemed like the light went out of there.
My father had so much beauty, so much light, and so much joy in his eyes.
And so did my mother.
But some kind of, like a film, came over their eyes.
There was laughter then but not quite the way it used to be before.
I imagined, and I understood that losing a child was the most horrible thing in a parent's life.
Still we tried to continue our lives in keeping a semblance of normalcy in the house because there were still four other children.
But I could see, even when my father smiled, when they listened to music, there was always some sadness that settled into their eyes, into their hearts, which was projected through their eyes.
It wasn't quite the same.
And then, of course, in 1941, the Germans broke the treaty with Russia.
And they attacked Russia.
And the Germans came into my hometown.
I remember it very distinctly.
Whatever was happening in other parts, in our town, it was very quiet when the first Germans showed up.
Later on, of course, there was a great welcome for them.
But I remember it was very quiet in town.
And then I went to the center to see what was happening because I was a very curious girl.
And I saw a cannon with six German soldiers roar from the side of the street into the center.
And I stood looking at them.
And I say to myself, God, is this the German might?
What can they do with six soldiers and a cannon?
I was 11 years old.
And this is how I perceived them.
I didn't know what was going to follow.
But very soon after they settled in, they ask all men from the age of 18 to 50 to come to the police for registration.
My father fell under that age category.
And he went to the registration.
We were expecting him home for dinner.
But my father didn't come home that night.
He didn't come home the following day.

And then rumor started flying all over the ghetto that 600 leading citizens who were detained at the registration, because some of them were let go, were taken into the forest and massacred into open graves.
For me, it was very difficult to accept that my father was just shot.
You see, my father was a hero in World War I.
He saved 38 high ranking officers, German officers from the Russian Cossacks.
So I thought maybe my father tried to escape.
Maybe he was wounded while escaping.
So I went into the forest looking for him and calling his name the whole day.
And then I was very tired.
And I fell asleep at the edge of the forest.
And my brother Zachary found me.
He lifted me into his arms.
And he said, Alicia, our father is not coming home.
I was 11 years old.
And that's when my childhood stopped.
And my father never came home.
In 1967, I was asked to come to German consul--
My mother was a Swedish subject.
She still had her citizenship.
It wasn't that easy to leave Poland because we were such a big family.
My grandfather sent a lot of money to Switzerland to get to the Swedish consul, to get the whole family out.
But you can't only a few people left.
The whole family had to leave together.
And people wouldn't really have let my grandfather go.
He was supporting so many people.
He was in charge of so many people.
I don't really know what went on.
But I know they tried to get out.
Because during the Russian occupation, they couldn't get out.
But when the war started in '39, they tried to get out.
But it isn't just getting out.
The question is nobody would let them in.
I'm constantly asked how come a family like ours didn't get out.
To get out, you have to have some place to go.
And the countries closed their doors to us.
